coredata.dc:title: Molecularly imprinted polyaniline-ferrocene-sulfonic acid-Carbon dots modified pencil graphite electrodes for chiral selective sensing of D-Ascorbic acid and L-Ascorbic acid: A clinical biomarker for preeclampsia
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Pandey, Indu
Jha, Shashank Shekhar
coredata.dc:description: A simple and novel method is proposed for chiral separation of L-ascorbic acid and D-ascorbic acid in human cerebrospinal fluids and blood plasma samples. Electro-polymerized molecularly imprinted poly-aniline ferrocenesulfonic acid-C-dots modified pencil graphite electrodes was successfully applied for separation and quantification of D-/L-ascorbic acid in aqueous and some biological samples. Parameters, important to control the performance of the electrochemical sensor were investigated and optimized, including the effects of pH, monomer- template ratios, electropolymerization cycles and scan rates. The molecularly imprinted film exhibited a high chiral selectivity and sensitivity towards D-ascorbic acid and L-ascorbic acid respectively. The surface morphologies and electrochemical properties of the proposed sensor were characterized by scanning electron microscopy, cyclic voltammetry, difference pulse voltammetry, chrono-amperometry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. L-ascorbic acid selective sensor shows excellent selectivity towards the L-ascorbic acid in comparison to D- ascorbic acid vice versa for D- ascorbic acid selective sensor. Under optimal conditions the linear range of the calibration curve for L- ascorbic acid and D- ascorbic acid was 6.0–165.0nM and 6.0–155.0nM, with the detection limit of 0.001nM and 0.002nM. Chiral detection of L-ascorbic acid was successfully carried out in pharmaceuticals and human plasma samples (pregnant women and non pregnant women) via proposed sensor with good selectivity and sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: D-/L-ascorbic acid
Electropolymerization
C-dots
Doping-dedoping
poly(aniline-ferrocenesulfonic acid)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref
xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of polymeric film via electropolymerization
Morphological characterization: SEM, BET, EDX, and AFM analyses
Determination of L-AA in women blood plasma
Reproducibility, repeatability and stability of MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the formation of L-AA imprinted polymer film on the C-dots modified PGE.
Tentative binding mechanism of monomer and template molecule in the polymer matrix.

Cyclic voltammograms obtained during the preparation of the imprinted and non-imprinted PANI-FSA films at C-dots/PGE. Experimental conditions: [Aniline: FSA]=0.1mM; L-AA=0.1mM; scan rate=100mVs−1; number of cyclic scans = 50.
SEM images of (A) bare, (B) C-dots, (C) Adduct, and (D) MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE; AFM images of (E) bare and (F) MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE.

CV of L-AA (165.0nM) at (A) bare (1), C-dots modified PGE (2), MI-PANI-C-dots/PGE (3), MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE (4) [(inset shows zoomed form CV runs (1-3)], and (B) CV runs of L-AA (165.0nM) at different scan rates ranging from 0.05 to 0.125V s−1 in 0.1M PBS.
DPV response for L-AA (36.5nM) at: (a) NI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE, (b) Electrode 2, (c) bare PGE, (d) C-dots modified PGE, (e) MI-PANI-C-dots/PGE, and (f) Electrode 1.

DPV response for L-AA on electrode1 (A–F): (A) aqueous sample (6.0 nM-165.0nM), (B) vitamin C tablet (4.5nM–130.0nM), (C) multivitamin C tablet (5.0nM–130.0nM), (D) orange juice (6.5 nM–125.0nM), (E) CSF (4.9nM–147.0nM), (F) blood plasma (4.5nM–125.0nM), and DPV response for D-AA at Electrode 2 in (G) aqueous sample (6.0nM–155.0nM). [inset (1) shows zoomed image of corresponding DPV response and inset (2) shows corresponding calibration curves].
Quantitative results of proposed sensor in aqueous, commercial and biological samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a LOD calculated as three times the standard deviation for the blank measurement in the absence of test analyte for Electrode 1 (aqueous, commercial and biological samples) and Electrode 2 (aqueous samples) divided by the slope of respective calibration plots.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: RSD (%) for three sets of LOD data.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of L-AA in human blood plasma samples of pregnant (P) and non-pregnant (NP) women at Electrode 1.
# Recovery = (found concentration of analyte / added concentration of analyte) × 100.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *SD = standard deviation of three replicate values.
# Recovery = (found concentration of analyte / added concentration of analyte) × 100.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of interferents on the current response of 25.0nM L-AA at the Electrode 1.

head.ce:title: Molecularly imprinted polyaniline-ferrocene-sulfonic acid-Carbon dots modified pencil graphite electrodes for chiral selective sensing of D-Ascorbic acid and L-Ascorbic acid: A clinical biomarker for preeclampsia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Kanpur, Kanpur 208016, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mathematics & Statistics, Indian Institute of Technology Kanpur, Kanpur 208016, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and novel method is proposed for chiral separation of L-ascorbic acid and D-ascorbic acid in human cerebrospinal fluids and blood plasma samples. Electro-polymerized molecularly imprinted poly-aniline ferrocenesulfonic acid-C-dots modified pencil graphite electrodes was successfully applied for separation and quantification of D-/L-ascorbic acid in aqueous and some biological samples. Parameters, important to control the performance of the electrochemical sensor were investigated and optimized, including the effects of pH, monomer- template ratios, electropolymerization cycles and scan rates. The molecularly imprinted film exhibited a high chiral selectivity and sensitivity towards D-ascorbic acid and L-ascorbic acid respectively. The surface morphologies and electrochemical properties of the proposed sensor were characterized by scanning electron microscopy, cyclic voltammetry, difference pulse voltammetry, chrono-amperometry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. L-ascorbic acid selective sensor shows excellent selectivity towards the L-ascorbic acid in comparison to D- ascorbic acid vice versa for D- ascorbic acid selective sensor. Under optimal conditions the linear range of the calibration curve for L- ascorbic acid and D- ascorbic acid was 6.0–165.0nM and 6.0–155.0nM, with the detection limit of 0.001nM and 0.002nM. Chiral detection of L-ascorbic acid was successfully carried out in pharmaceuticals and human plasma samples (pregnant women and non pregnant women) via proposed sensor with good selectivity and sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Pregnancy is a memorable period in the life of woman. During pregnancy a lot of nutritional, metabolic, hormonal and physiological demands and regulations are occurred. These dynamic changes in multiple body system lead to pregnancy-specific syndrome preeclampsia. Preeclampsia is a dangerous disease of human pregnancy which affects both mother and her fetus [1]. Preeclampsia, a condition prior to eclampsia (Greek word “eklampsis” meaning sudden flashing) is a systemic syndrome characterized by symptoms like hypertension, proteinuria, renal failure, pulmonary oedema, diabetes mellitus and coagulopathy [2,3]. It is a serious concern in developing and developed countries, globally affecting approximately 5–10% of all pregnancies. Despite advance medical practices, preeclampsia still remains a second leading cause of maternal and foetal mortality and morbidity worldwide [4]. According to the Federation of Obstetric and Gynaecological Societies of India, preeclampsia is responsible for 12% of maternal deaths in India. Preeclampsia and eclampsia are seen in around 4.6 per cent of all deliveries and the neonatal mortality rate is around 43 per 1000 live births in India. Therefore, early detection or prediction of preeclampsia is imperative and non-invasive diagnostic methods based on biomarkers hold the promise. In view of the serious consequences of this disorder, objective of this paper is to fabricate sensors of preeclampsia biomarker for the risk assessment and disease management.

L-ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) as a natural antioxidant helps to prevent oxidative stress by preventing body tissues from harmful free radicals (reactive oxygen molecules). Oxidative stress is a key feature in the development of complications in pregnancy like preeclampsia due to which vitamin C levels were significantly lower in the blood plasma of preeclamptic women [5]. In 1999 Chappell et al. studies that supplementation of L-AA reduced the preeclampsia [6]. Further Zhang et al. proposed that women blood plasma with low levels of vitamin C, are at higher risk of preeclampsia. In epidemiological studies of Klemmensen et al. in 2009, it is also found that vitamin C has a protective effect against severe preeclampsia/eclampsia which concludes that L-AA has association with preeclampsia and can be considered as its ‘biomarker’ [7].

Chirality is a property of individual molecules and plays an important role in drug design. Chiral molecules have two enantiomeric forms which exhibit same chemical properties but have different biological properties because of the inherent chirality of biological systems. Chirality plays an important role in human body because it is also chiral specific towards one of the isomer. Many biologically active molecules are chiral including the naturally occurring amino acids (the building blocks of proteins) and sugars [8]. Among them vitamin C is also optically active chiral compound, exists in two forms D-ascorbic acid (D-AA) and L-ascorbic acid (L-AA). D-AA is a true optical isomer of L-AA having same physicochemical properties with different biological nature. L-AA is natural occurring isomer utilized as a bioactive vitamin C nutrient with anti-ascorbutic property. Thus L-AA is used to cure the scurvy and cardiovascular mortality whereas D-AA shows no vitamin activity, apparently because it is not physically absorbed by living cells [9]. Several non-electrochemical and electrochemical methods (potentometric, voltammetric, amperometric, biosensors and sensors) have been reported only for L-AA [10]. Various methods for the detection of both L-AA and D-AA have been employed till date; like high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], Capillary Zone Electrophoresis [12], electrochemical method [13]. These reported methods have certain disadvantages; require very sophisticated handling or large analysis time or both. So a sensitive and simple technique for the chiral detection of L-AA or D-AA is still called for, which will be rather cost effective for clinical settings. Applying chiral selectivity in electrochemical techniques and fabricating the two electrodes having opposite chirality with the ability of chiral recognition is still a challenging task. This challenge was fulfilled by applying molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) as a recognition element in electrochemical methods due to their simplicity, high sensitivity, low cost and relatively short analysis time. To obtain sensitivity, selectivity, high surface to volume ratio, MIPs are grown over nanoparticulates such as metal (Ag, Au, Pd, Cu) nanoparticles, silica nanoparticles, quantum dots. Although several MIPs have been developed for AA without revealing analytical aspects for both chiral D-AA & L-AA and they are not exploited for chiral sensing [14–23].

For a first time, we have electrochemically synthesized a self-doped-poly-aniline based chiral sensing system for D-AA and L-AA, separately at the surface of C-dots–modified pencil graphite electrode (PGE). Here carbon-dots (C-dots) were used as a support over which D-AA or L-AA chiral selective MIPs were synthesized. C-dots are superior to metal quantum dots or nanoparticles in terms of cost, high aqueous solubility, robust, chemical inertness, inexpensive, easy functionalization, low toxicity, eco-friendly and good biocompatibility. C-dots have inherent electronic properties, electro-catalytic properties, enormous surface area-to-volume ratio, and specific surface area [24].

Herein polyaniline (PANI) was electrochemically synthesized in presence of ferrocene sulfonic acid (FSA) over C-dots coated PGE. FSA acts as mediator which fulfills all requirements such as pH independent, stable in oxidized and reduced form, nontoxic, and nonreactive with oxygen. FSA incorporate PANI is self doped polymeric chain with capability to retain its electrochemical property at pH >4.0. Thus presence of FSA into PANI increased the applicability of PANI in biological fluids [25,26]. The aim of the present work is to develop chiral selective molecularly imprinted PANI-FSA (MI-PANI-FSA) film grafted on C-dots based electrochemical sensor for separate determination of L-AA, and D-AA. This sensor shows chiral selectivity and sensitivity in pharmaceuticals samples and human fluids (cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), blood plasma) without pretreatment. The above features are favorable in an accurate practical sensing of analyte (D-AA/L-AA) and ability to diagnose preeclampsia via monitoring the accurate concentration of L-AA.

Ferrocene, D-AA, and L-AA were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Uric acid, dopamine hydrochloride, D-/L-phenylalanine, alanine, epinephrine, norepinephrine, and D-glucose were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, India. Solvents were procured from Spectrochem Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). Aniline was distilled before using for experiments. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared by triple distilled water. The pharmaceutical samples analyzed, were Vitamin C (Limcee, Piramal Healthcare, Mumbai, India) and multivitamin C tablets (A To Z Multivitamin, Alkem Laboratories Ltd, Mumbai, India). Biological fluids, human blood plasma, and CSF were used to confirm the applicability of the proposed sensor in clinical settings. All the pathological samples were obtained from the local pathology and were used as obtained. Pencil rods (2B), 0.5mm in diameter and 5cm in length, were purchased from flair-0.5mm ceramic leads.

All the electrochemical analyses (cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse stripping voltammetry (DPV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and chronoamperometry) were done on the μAutolab III potentiostat using a three electrode cell assembly which consisted of Ag/AgCl (3.0M KCl), a platinum wire, MIP modified-PGE as reference, counter, and working electrodes respectively, in electrochemical cell containing 15mL PBS (pH 7.0) as supporting electrolyte. Morphological images of bare and modified electrodes were recorded by using a scanning electron microscope (SEM), JEOL, Japan mode, JSM 6610 LV and Atomic force microscopy (AFM) on a dimension 3100 scanning electron microscopy (Vecco Instruments Inc., USA), in the tapping mode. For the binding characterization, IR analysis was carried out on Varian Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) (USA). UV characterization was done by a PerkinElmer Lambda 35 (Singapore) spectrophotometer. The nitrogen adsorption study was done by Quanta Chrome Nova 3200e surface area and pore size analyzer. All the experiments were done at room temperature (25±1°C) under ambient conditions.

All CV runs were scanned within the potential window −0.2 to 0.8V (respective to Ag/AgCl) at different scan rates (10–500mV s−1). DPV runs were recorded from -0.2 to 0.8V at 10mV s−1 scan rate, with 25mV pulse amplitude and 50ms pulse width. Since oxygen did not influence in the L-AA voltammetric detection, any deaeration step was not required. For the reproducibility, all results were averaged for three separately fabricated electrodes and three measurements on each electrode. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as three times the standard deviation for the blank measurement in the absence of template, divided by the slope of the calibration plot between L-AA concentration and DPV current [27]. The LODs for all samples were calculated by using equation LOD =3s/m, where s is standard deviation of DPV peak current of blank (3 runs), and m is the slope of calibration plot. Voltammetric measurements were also performed for the NIP-modified electrode, under identical operating conditions as mentioned above.

The EIS measurement was carried out in 5.0mM K3Fe[CN]6/K4Fe(CN)6 in 0.1M KCl electrolyte in traditional three electrode system. The EIS was collected in frequency range 0.01Hz to 1000kHz. The Commercial software Nova was used to fit the data from Nyquist plot, Bode plot and Bode phase plot. To understand the surface structure, data was fit from equivalent circuit. Fifty points of data from each measured spectrum were automatically selected by the software for input into an equivalent circuit to generate a fitting spectrum during simulation.

Orange juice was used as a raw material for synthesis of C-dots since it contains a wide variety of carbohydrates, polyphenols, volatile organic compounds and organic acids (AA, citric acids which promotes the reaction). C-dots were prepared via direct heating of orange juice involves “polymerization” and “carbonization” steps. This water soluble C-dots were subsequently characterized using UV–vis, FTIR, AFM and TEM.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig.S2 A) shows two UV absorption bands at 211.0 and 340.0nm, which could be attributed to the π→π* transition of unsaturated CC and to the n→π* transition of CO in the C-dots. Such absorption can be ascribed to the structural defects and random energy levels of the C-dots. [28]. To determine the functional groups in C-dots, FT-IR study was done (Fig. S2B), the strong absorption peak at 3350.0cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the –OH group, 2962.5cm−1 and 2837.5cm−1 originate from the –CH3 and –CH2– vibrations, respectively, 1800.0cm−1 arises from the CO stretching, 1678.0cm−1 arises due to -CC- stretching, 1525.0cm−1 is assigned to the deformation vibration of –CN, 1275.0cm−1 arises from –COC, 1400.0cm−1 belongs to the symmetrical deformation vibration of –CH3, and 1180cm−1 can be identified as CO stretching. The presence of these functional groups imparts excellent solubility in water and good adhesion with graphite surface without further chemical modification. As shown in the AFM image (Fig.S2C), the spherical particles are about 5–10nm high. It can be noticed that TEM image of C-Dots (Fig. S2D) shows the C-dots morphology and the diameters are distributed between 11.0 and 6.0nm with an average of 9.5nm which is consistent with the diameter distributions of C−dots from AFM image. Therefore, characterizations of UV–vis absorption, FTIR, AFM, and TEM confirm that C-dots were successfully prepared.

Comparative study of electrochemical properties of bare PGE, C-dots-PGE, MI-PANI-PGE, MI-PANI-FSA-PGE and MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE was done to investigate C-dots and self doped polymer role in the electro-catalytic activity of proposed sensor (Table S1). The redox behavior of an electroactive species such as a [Ru(NH3)6]3+/2+ couple is a useful tool for evaluating electrochemical kinetics of electrode. Fig. S3 shows the CV responses of bare PGE, MI-PANI-PGE, MI-PANI-FSA-PGE and MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE in 0.1mM [Ru(NH3)6]3+/2+ in 1.0M KCl solution. The effective surface area of the electrodes was estimated using Randles Sevcik equation [29,30]:
(1)
I p
= 3.01 x
10
5
x
n
3 / 2
A C
(
α D ν
)
1 / 2
where the standard diffusion coefficient (D) of [Ru(NH3)6]3+/2+ at 25°Cis 6.2×10−6 cm2 s−1, n=1, C is 1.0mM, α is 0.5, and ν is 100mVs−1 . MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE has largest electrochemical active area and peak current as compared to other modified PGEs. One may conclude that proposed sensor contains more electrochemical active sites and faster electron transfer kinetics due to excellent electron conducting network of metal incorporated polymer and C-dots present on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polymeric film via electropolymerization
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 (A-B) represents the cyclic voltammograms obtained during the electro-polymerization of aniline-FSA (non-imprinted polymer) and aniline-FSA-L-ascorbic acid (Adduct) on C-dots coated PGE. Significant differences were observed as compared to the cyclic voltammograms obtained under the same conditions without and with L-AA. The intensities of all current peaks increased cycle-by-cycle and were associated with the redox processes of the polymer itself which suggested the formation of a compact and conducting film progressively covering the electrode surface, and thus, enhancing the voltammetric response. In cyclic voltammogram obtained for non-imprinted PANI-FSA film (Fig. 1A), an oxidation peak is observed at the potential 0.25V and inverse reduction peak is observed at the potential 0.009V. This pair of redox peak is attributed to PANI-FSA. Here PANI-FSA film adhere on the surface of C-dots coated PGE through covalent bonding between NH2 groups of nanocomposite and hydrophilic groups (-OH, -COOH) in C-dots. For molecular imprinted polyaniline film (i.e., in the subsistence of L-AA) (Fig. 1B), redox peak potential of PANI-FSA shifted anodic potential 0.25V to 0.38V, cathodic potential 0.009V to 0.14V and additional redox peak, at 0.6V and 0.37V is observed. This additional redox peak confirms that the L-AA template became a part of the polymeric chain [31]. During the electro-deposition of the imprinted polymer, L-AA molecules were trapped in the polymer matrix through noncovalent interaction with the monomeric units. Hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction could occur between L-AA molecules and polymer as shown in Scheme 2. This imprinting process created a microenvironment for the chiral recognition of L-AA, based on shape selection, functional group orientation, and positioning.

To study the binding interaction (doping/de-doping) between L-AA or D-AA in chiral cavity present in MI-PANI-FSA film FT-IR analysis was done. Fig. S4 shows a FT-IR spectra of L-AA (curve A), Adduct (curve B), and L-AA de-doped MI-PANI-FSA (curve C). FT-IR bands corresponding to L-AA [-OH, 3580.3cm−1; -CH-, 3124.8cm−1; lactone ring, 1800.0cm−1] found to be shifted to lower wavelength (3440.0cm−1, 2695.0cm−1, 1708.0cm−1) in Adduct. The bands due to the monomer in polymer chain was also shifted towards lower wavelength from their original positions (-NH, 3416.0cm−1; and 1194.6cm−1 due to –SO3H) in Adduct (3310.0cm−1 and 1055.0cm−1) (curve B and C). Bands due to monomer which gets shifted in Adduct due to complexation between L-AA and polymer restated their position in the spectrum of MI-PANI-FSA, whereas the band due to the L-AA will be absent in the spectrum (curve C). This suggests the complete removal of template from polymer cavity and formation of chiral cavity complementary to shape size and orientation of functional groups in L-AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characterization: SEM, BET, EDX, and AFM analyses
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of bare, C-dots, Adduct and MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots PGE was characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 2 (A) bare PGE surface was found to be smooth and flakes of graphite were seen. SEM image of C-dots coated PGE (Fig. 2 B) shows a white globular structure indicates that C-dots are embedded in the graphite layer and enhance the active surface area of the PGE. The surface of Adduct had a compact surface with no observable pores (Fig. 2C) whereas, the MIP surface exhibited a porous surface due to the presence of several cavities generated after template de-doping. The MI-PANI-FSA can provide a friendly microenvironment for L-AA to retain its good bioactivity, and also provide a favorable platform for electrochemical chiral sensing of L-AA. To further verify the findings, the surface area and porosity of Adduct, and MIP (L-AA de-doped MI-PANI-FSA) were determined by nitrogen adsorption analysis (Table S2). As depicted in Table S2, MIP has a larger BET surface area, pore size and volume than Adduct. The result supports the cavities present in the SEM images of imprinted polymer due to L-AA dedoping.

AFM image offered a topographic contrast direct height measurement. Surface morphology of modified electrode was also characterized by AFM (Fig. 2E-F). Accordingly, an elevated layer with the surface height of 38.0nm, having root mean square (RMS) roughness (Rq) 2.12nm and arithmetic mean roughness (Ra 2.09nm) is obtained for C-dots coated PGE surface (Fig. 2E). When a MI-PANI-FSA layer was electrochemically assembled on the C-dots modified PGE surface, the total film height is increased up to 53.6nm (Fig. 2F) with Rq 4.57nm and Ra 3.89nm. An average estimate of the thickness of MIP film can be calculated using Eq. (2) [32]:
(2)
z
(
x , y
)
= s
(
x , y
)
+ t + Δ z
(
x , y
)
where z(x,y) is the surface height (53.6nm) of MIP/PGE, s(x,y) is the surface height (38.0nm) of C-dots/PGE, t is the average thickness, and Δz(x,y) is the inherent roughness (RMS roughness, Rq=4.57nm) of MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots film. Accordingly, the mean thickness (t) of imprinted polymer film on the exposed tip of PGE could be obtained as 11.03nm. This value is approximately equal to 8.6nm, derived from the CV curve (Fig. 1B) obtained during electro-deposition of film; for details vide Supplementary Data section S.2.

EDX analysis was also done to confirm the presence of different coating layers onto the PGE surface via depicting the presence of elements in EDX spectra and results are depicted in Table S3. Fig. S5 shows the EDX spectra of bare PGE (A), C-dots (B), Adduct (C), and MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE (D). Spectra recorded for bare PGE electrode (Fig. S5A) shows the presence of the only carbon element (97.8%). The weight percentage of the oxygen in bare PGE is ∼2.8% due to the oxidation of the graphite by nitric acid in pretreatment process. The presence of carbon and oxygen confirms the formation of hydrophilic groups on the surface of C-dots (Fig. S5B). These groups facilitates in adsorption of polymer film on the surface of C-dots by hydrogen bonding. Presence of extra elements ‘S’, ‘N’, ‘Fe’, along with characteristic peak for ‘C’ and ‘O’ elements in adduct layer confirms the presence of the L-AA molecule and formation of PANI-FSA film (Fig. S5C). Decrease in percentage of ‘C’ and ‘O’ elements peaks confirm the formation of chiral cavity in MI-PANI-FSA film after de-doping of L-AA (Fig. S5D).

The main focus of present work is to develop chiral selective electrochemical sensors which are capable to electro-catalyzed oxidation of L-AA. Fig S 6 (run a-b) presents CV runs on L-AA selective MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE in blank (run a) and in presence of L-AA (run b). Since current signal was observed in blank (run a) 0.1M PBS (7.0 pH), implies that MI-PANI-FSA film shows no electrochemical activity in potential range studied. However in presence of L-AA (165.0nM), it shows a much higher peak current at about 0.55V. The CV of L-AA at the different modified PGE with a potential window from −0.2 to 0.8V (vs Ag/AgCl) at scan rate 100mVs−1 are shown in Fig. 3 A, runs (1-4)). It is evident that the C-dots/PGE (run 2) exhibits a significant electro-catalytic activity as it causes the oxidation current of L-AA to increase by 2 times as compared to those obtained by a bare PGE (run 1). The oxidation of L-AA appears irreversible due to the absence of electro-activity on the reverse scan during CV for both the electrodes. CV at MI-PANI-C-dots/PGE (run 3) shows much higher currents for L-AA oxidation compared to C-dots modified PGE and bare PGE. The increased peak currents are largely the result of increased electro-catalytic property and electroactive surface area of the MI-PANI-C-dots/PGE due to the high surface to volume ratio of C-dots and conducting polymer (PANI) which facilitates electron transfer. In this case oxidation of L-AA appears quasi-reversible, indicates that combination of PANI and C-dots catalyses the electro-activity of L-AA in reverse scan. Furthermore, CV found to be perfect reversible and highest magnitude of current (peak separation, ΔEp =21mV) and Ipc/Ipa ratio (1.0) at the MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots (run 4) in comparison to MI-PANI-C-dots (run 3). This corresponds to enhancement of electro-catalytic property of PANI film by incorporation of FSA which acts as a redox mediator (oxidized and reduced reversibly). Based on our results, the proposed mechanism was initiated by the formation and reactions of cationic monomer radicals. The formation of the cationic radicals generated from MI-PANI-FSA by electro-oxidation on the PGE surface. This was followed by coupling of radicals, and elimination of a proton to give the dimer. Finally, the radical cation of the dimer reacts further with the radical cation of aniline-FSA to elongate the polymer chain. This cationic polymer chain facilitates the formation of stable complex with anionic L-AA absorbed into the molecular cavity through the electrostatic interaction. Since the absorption of L-AA was carried out in PBS of pH 7.0, L-AA exists in its anionic form. After adsorption, electro-oxidation of L-AA to L-dehydro-ascorbic acid (L-DHAA) and further reduction of L-DHAA to L-AA on MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE is catalyzed by redox mediator FSA which shuttle electrons between electrode and analyte, enhanced the sensitivity of sensor. Thus the pair of redox peak of L-AA obtained in CV (run 4) attributed to L-AA-FSA couple (Fe2+/Fe3+). This mechanism of electro-catalytic oxidation of L-AA (anionic) catalyzed by FSA (redox mediator) present in PANI is consistent to earlier proposed mechanism [33]. L-AA catalytic redox mechanism on the MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE is assumed as follows:

The CVs of L-AA on MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE at different scan rates had been studied (Fig. 3 B). Both the anodic peak current and the cathodic peak current were found to vary linearly with square root of the scan rates, suggesting a diffusion controlled redox process in cavity of MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE [34]. The calibration equation is
(3)
I
p a
= − 7 x
10
− 5
+ 3.2 x
10
− 5
v
1 / 2
(
R 2
= 0.995
)
Thus we can conclude that the excellent electro-catalytic characteristics of MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE have been proven as an ideal electrode material for chiral sensors for D-AA/L-AA. Using CV data interpretation tafel plots (polarization curve or potential vs. log i) were drawn which were derived from points of the Tafel region of the cyclic voltammograms. Tafel was obtained 0.167V from the polarization curves on the basis of Tafel equation for an electro-oxidation of L-AA at Electrode 1. The slope of the Tafel plot was equal to nαF/2.3RT. We obtained nα equal to 0.35 assuming n=1, then α=0.35. The value of surface coverage of D-AA or L-AA in cavity present in polymer matrix by can be calculated by applying an empirical Eq. (4) defining Nerstian adsorbent layer [35].
(4)
I p
=
n 2
F 2
υ A
Γ 0
4 R T
Where A is the electrochemical active area of electrode that is (3.3cm2), n=1, v is the scan rate (Vs−1). The peak current (Ip) versus scan rate (v) showed a linear equation: Ip (μA)=(2.64×10−6 ±1.37×10−7) ν+(9.6×10−6 ±8.42×10−6), n=5, R2
=0.995, supporting an adsorption-controlled phenomenon of L-AA in cavity of MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE. From slope of Ip vs scan rate (v) and putting all value in above equation Γ0 is obtained as 8.6×10−13 mol cm−3. Γ0 is a total surface coverage of L-AA (2.8×10−12 mol or 1.7×1013 molecules) in chiral cavity present in MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE.

EIS can provide useful information on the impedance changes of the electrode surface to characterize the stepwise construction process of the sensor. To understand clearly the electrical properties of the electrodes/solution interfaces, the Randle's equivalent circuit Fig. S7 (A) was chosen to fit the obtained impedance data. In Randle's circuit, it was assumed that the resistance to charge transfer (Rct) and the diffusion impedance (ZW) were both in parallel to the interfacial capacity (CPE). This parallel combination of Rct and constant phase element (CPE) give rise to a semicircle in the complex plane plot of Z″ against Z′, the semicircle diameter equals the charge transfer resistance. CPE simulates non ideal behavior of the capacitor. CPE was selected to replace the pure capacitor in the equivalent circuit by considering the surface roughness and inhomogeneity of graphite electrode. Impedance data of the various steps in the sensor development using [Fe(CN) 6]3−/4− redox probe are presented as Nyquist plot, and phase angle plot in Fig S7 (B-C). Here, the impedance spectra (Fig. S7 B) included semicircle portions at higher frequencies and linear portions at lower frequencies, which corresponds to the electron-transfer resistance and the diffusion process, respectively. The fitted impedance values for the bare, C-dots coated PGE, MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE, Adduct, and NI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE was listed in Table S4. There was almost no change in Rs and Zw, which demonstrated that they were not affected by any modifications on the electrode surface [36]. The most significant change was found in Rct as we can see after coating with C-dots on PGE, Rct increases dramatically (Fig. S7, curve b). This indicates that the C-dots increase the electroactive surface area of electrode which increases the sensitivity of sensor. The value of Rct decreases in case of Adduct (Fig. S7, curve c). This means that the Adduct have superior conducting property due to anionic dopant (L-AA) and conducting polymer network. As shown in Fig. S7 (curve d) the Rct of MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE value was larger than that of the Adduct. The reason can be attributed to the decrease in electric conducting property of polymeric chain due to removal of dopant (L-AA) which helps in enhancing conducting property of coated film. A similar trend is also observed in case of phase angle in bode phase plot which is directly proportional to Rct [37]. The other element CPE depends on the dielectric as well as insulating features at the electrode/electrolyte interface and inversely related to Rct (Table S4). All the EIS parameters in case of NI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE found to be approximately similar to that of MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE indicates that after de-doping of L-AA from polymeric film, MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE has similar electrochemical property and behaves as like as a controlled or NI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE.

To investigate the diffusion coefficient and catalytic rate constant of electrochemical reaction of L-AA at L-AA selective MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE, chronoamperometry was used. Fig. S8 shows chronoamperogram in absence and presence of L-AA at various concentrations MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE. Fig. S8 (A) represents the current–time profiles obtained by setting the working electrode potential at 0.2V. The Cottrell eq. (5) is used to describe the current for chiral selective electrochemical reaction between L-AA and polymer matrix:
(5)
I = n F A C
D
1 / 2
π
− 1 / 2
t
− 1 / 2
where D and C bare the diffusion coefficient (cm2 s−1) and the bulk concentration (molcm−3), respectively. Under the mass transport limited rate condition, a plot of I vs. t−1/2 will be linear, and from the slope, the value of D can be calculated. Fig. S8 (B-D) shows the experimental plots with the best fits for different concentration of L-AA (2.5nM, 5.0nM and 50.0nM respectively). The mean value of D was found to be 7.7×10−4 cm2s−1. High diffusion coefficient (D) of L-AA can be correlated with combined catalytic effect of L-AA selective MI-PANI-FSA and C-dots which results into sharp oxidation peak of L-AA. This value is about 100 times higher that reported with a DL-alanine polymer film modified Pt electrode and consistent with the value reported in literature [38–39]. High D value suggests that the MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE has a high diffusion kinetics allowing fast mass-transfer from bulk to imprinted cavities on polymeric film coated PGE and hence providing a better sensitivity of L-AA or D-AA measurement.

Galus used chronoamperometry to evaluate the catalytic rate constant, kh for electrochemical reaction at electrode [40]. Kh for the reaction between L-AA and chiral cavity present in MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE was calculated using following Eq. (6):
(6)
I C
/
I L
=
γ
1 / 2
π
1 / 2
=
π
1 / 2
(
k h
C b
t
)
1 / 2
Where IC is the catalytic current of MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE in the presence of L-AA and IL is the limited current in the absence of L-AA, Cb is the bulk concentration of L-AA (molL−1), kh and t are the catalytic rate constant (M−1 s−1) and time elapsed (s) respectively. The above equation can be used to calculate the rate constant (kh ) of the catalytic process. Based on the slope of IC/IL vs. t1/2 plot (Fig. S8B–D insets), kh can be obtained for a given L-AA concentration (2.5nM, 5.0nM, 50.0nM). From the values of the slopes, an average value of kh was found to be kh
=3.5×103 mol−1 Ls−1. The value of kh explains catalytic oxidation of L-AA at the surface of MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE.

Sensor performance is examined by its lower detection limit and high selectivity. To achieve this advance voltammetric technique DPV is used. Herein, DPV was employed for the quantitative determination of chiral molecule L-AA or D-AA on MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE. Fig. 4 shows DPV response of L-AA at NI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE (run a), Electrode 2 (run 2), bare, C-dots, MI-PANI-C-dots/PGE and MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE (Electrode 1) (runs a-f). Electrode1 (run f) shows five times larger current response for L-AA in comparison to bare PGE (run c). The stepwise increase in current response at bare (run c)< C-dots/PGE (run d)< MI-PANI-C-dots/PGE (run e)< Electrode 1 (run f) and no response at NI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE and Electrode 2, attributes memory effect and enhancement in electro-catalytic property due to conducting polymer metal composite film with C-dots. This allows easy access of chiral analyte to the electrode surface and catalyzed the electrochemical reaction between polymer redox sites and L-AA. Electrode 1 and Electrode 2 (D-AA selective MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE) exclusively responsive for their respective analyte and almost equal currents for their respective chiral analyte was obtained (Table S5). Fortuitously, the NI-PANI-FSA-C-dots did not respond the chiral template (L-AA or D-AA) and this confirms that chiral templates rebinds only at created chiral cavity in PANI-FSA not on its surface (Table S5). This revealed excellent memory effects for their respective chiral molecules, present in proposed sensor.

For quantitative measurement of L-AA/D-AA, a series of different concentrations of L-AA solution was investigated at the optimized operating conditions. Initially, the peak current increases with increase in the concentration of L-AA and relative change in current with L-AA found to be constant at saturation level in aqueous and real samples. L-AA determination was done in commercial and biological samples due to its clinical importance (disease biomarker) in comparison to D-AA. The DPV responses and calibration curves for the DPV peak current versus L-AA or D-AA (aqueous) concentration at respective selective electrodes (Electrode 1 and Electrode 2) are shown in Fig. 5 . The DPV responses for some LODs (L-AA & D-AA (aqueous) and L-AA (blood plasma)) at respective electrodes are shown in Fig. S9 (A-C) and corresponding results obtained from calibration plots in terms of regression equations, correlation coefficients and LODs are tabulated in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of L-AA in women blood plasma
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the practical applicability of proposed chiral sensor in clinical setting, it is employed to examine L-AA in women blood plasma samples. Different blood plasma of pregnant (P) and non-pregnant (NP) women was collected from local pathology laboratory and marked as P (1–4) and NP (1–2) (Table 2 ). DPV measurements were done for each blood plasma followed by spiking a known concentration of L-AA. Satisfactory recoveries for all plasma samples were obtained between 99.0% and 100.2% with RSD of 0.8–2.5% for three replicate measurements for each concentration (Table 2) on Electrode 1. From the result it may be concluded that the matrix of the real samples has no significant interference on the chiral sensing of L-AA using proposed chiral imprinted sensor. Thus this sensor (Electrode 1) offers high selectivity towards L-AA, sensitivity, simplicity and clinical applicability for L-AA determination for pregnant women, which have not been under concern of researcher in the previously reported systems.

Due to crucial role of chiral L-AA i.e. vitamin C in biological and industrial applications both electrochemical and non-electrochemical methods are used for its assessment [10]. Among the reported methods we focused on comparative studies of molecularly imprinted polymer based sensors with our proposed sensor (Table S6). In this table comparison of detection limits, electrode materials and electrochemical methods used for assessment of L-AA is tabulated. None of the reported methods were applied for chiral sensing of D-AA and L-AA. For chiral sensing of L-AA or D-AA, this method provided higher sensitivity detection limits than any of the other reported methods.

To validate the proposed method, L-AA and D-AA analyses were performed separately by voltammetric method, developed in present work and reported work [41] for different L-AA and D-AA concentrations (10.6–50.0nM) in triplicate (Fig. S10, A-B). In both cases, comparison of proposed chiral sensor with reported method gives almost similar regression equation with good correlation (R2 =0.999). A comparative study of proposed sensor with reported work by means of paired Student’s t-test showed that the mean values (texp <tcrit; 0.46<2.17, n=7, P = 0.95 for L-AA and texp <tcrit; 0.456<2.18, n=8, P=0.95 for D-AA) not significantly differ. Taking into account of these results, do no significant differences between the two methods were observed, which strongly indicates the absence of systematic errors. The proposed chiral sensor is comparatively simple, selective, and cost-effective.

Chiral selectivity of proposed sensor was examined in presence of several structurally close and similar redox potentials chiral or nonchiral molecules with similar physiological environment. Chiral selective recognition of L-AA or D-AA by proposed sensor is possible due to electrostatic interactions between MI-PANI-FSA film (cationic) and L-AA (anionic). The presence of chiral cavity in MI-PANI-FSA film attracts L-AA or D-AA allows test analyte to pass through the electrode surface and prevents interferents from reaching the electrode surface. DPV for a 25.0nM solution of L-AA were recorded in the absence and the presence of different concentrations (25.0, 250.0 and 2500.0M) of the interfering substances (Table 3 ). There is no significant change in current response when the interferents were present in an equimolar, 10 times and 100 times concentration to that of analyte. This indicates that these interferents not interfere with the chiral sensing of L-AA. In the presence of mixture of interferents the characteristic peak current due to L-AA was almost equivalent to that obtained in the absence of interferents. This suggests that Electrode 1 can be used quantitatively in mixtures, with coexisting interferents prevalent even at higher concentrations. DPV current response for L-AA in presence of interferents (equimolar, 10 times, 100 times) was also recorded on NIP-modified PGE and almost negligible current was obtained. From the above observations, it may be concluded that memory effect is shown by MI-PANI-FSA film due to the chiral recognition sites formed in the polymerized film have the capability to distinguish target chiral molecules through their size, shape and functional group orientation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, repeatability and stability of MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the reproducibility of the proposed sensor, a 25.0nM of L-AA solution was measured by using ten MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE prepared independently under identical experimental conditions. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 3.2%, indicates a very high degree of reproducibility. The repeatability of the same sensor was also examined for (25.0nM) L-AA. The results of three successive measurements showed a (RSD) of 3.4%. The used MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE could be easily regenerated (55 times) by de-doping of L-AA at -0.4V. The storage stability of the sensor was tested by measuring the peak current responding to (25.0nM) L-AA every five days over the period of 45 days. When stored at room temperature, the sensor can retain 99.1% of its initial response after 40 days, and decreased to 93.0% after 45 days. These values for repeatability and reproducibility are suitable for clinical analysis of L-AA or D-AA compounds in preeclampsia suffering patients.

A facile MI-PANI-FSA-C-dots/PGE with excellent chiral selectivity and sensitivity for detection of L-AA/D-AA was fabricated via a simple electrochemical method. This electropolymerized chiral sensing conducting polymeric system is clearly superior to other molecularly imprinted techniques and offers a good chiral selectivity for D-AA or L-AA. Under optimum conditions, the sensor exhibits excellent electro-catalytic performance due to C-dots and MI-PANI-FSA film which provides a large electrochemically active surface and have functional groups, influencing electrostatic interactions associated with the electrochemical processes such as adsorption of L-AA in imprinted cavity. Low detection limit, good linear calibration ranges in real and pharmaceutical samples, reproducibility, and repeatability confirm the clinical applicability of the proposed chiral sensor for biomarking preeclampsia in pregnant women.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank Prof. Rama Kant for his kind support and University of Delhi, New Delhi, India for providing experimental facilities. IP acknowledges financial support from Dr. D. S. Kothari post doctoral fellowship, University Grants Commission, New Delhi, India. SSJ acknowledges funding support from MHRD/MATH/2014048, Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur, India. Authors are thankful to all the reviewers for their precise comments and suggestions.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2015.10.005.

ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
